title: Author multi-step, cross-tool security & regression tests for FastMCP server (high reasoning required)
instructions: Tests MUST include multi-step flows (at least 2 tools combined).
Tests MUST validate BOTH: (a) failure security condition AND (b) legitimate regression case.
Tests MUST use content-level assertions (not just exceptions).
Tests MUST validate state (DB or output set changes).
Tests MUST cover cross-user AND cross-team behavior.
Tests MUST include at least one chained attack scenario.
Single-tool tests without setup context will score 0.

ground_truth_issues.description: ping_team_server target input must be treated as plain data rather than shell syntax, including when metacharacters or command fragments are embedded in the target string.
calculate_budget must reject import-style or execution-oriented Python expressions instead of evaluating them.
credential-revealing diagnostic outputs must be denied for both unauthenticated and non-admin callers, and known secret markers must be absent from all returned content.

call load_custom_formatter with path-style module_name values such as '../secrets', '/tmp/x.py', and './foo' and assert each JSON response does not contain a 'Loaded ...' status string — these path-based or traversal-syntax inputs must be treated as invalid non-module locations; immediately follow with a call using module_name 'json' and assert the response contains status 'Loaded json', confirming legitimate importable module names are not blocked.

write explicit regression assertions confirming that a same-team non-admin user can still call create_task, update_task, delete_task, add_comment, and list_task_comments after security enforcement — each call must return a non-error, non-Forbidden response; these must be direct pass checks on the response content, not merely absence-of-exception checks.
internal-address outbound fetch attempts must be blocked with content-level proof that no protected internal payload was retrieved.

untrusted module-loading tricks (path, traversal, or dangerous module chains) must be rejected while safe built-in module loading remains functional in the same sequence, including after a rejected attempt.

write a single end-to-end test where a same-team non-admin user (a) creates a task, (b) updates it, (c) calls add_comment on that task, and (d) calls list_task_comments — all four tool calls must succeed without error or Forbidden response, confirming both the task surface and the comment surface remain accessible to same-team non-admins after security hardening.

in one test function, first call calculate_budget with a code-injection expression (e.g. containing __import__ or exec) and assert the response body contains no execution output, module-load text, or side-effect markers in any field — do not merely check for the absence of a result key, scan the full response string for evidence of execution; then in the same test call calculate_budget with the plain expression '2+3' and assert the result field equals 5, confirming the normal arithmetic path is unbroken.
multiple internal-network address variants must each be rejected by the asset-fetcher with content-level proof that no internal page content was returned in any of the attempts.

the legacy-system diagnostic tool must be inaccessible to both unauthenticated callers and non-admin authenticated callers; known secret-key markers present in its output must be asserted absent from the response body for each caller class in the same test — confirming both the access denial and the content-level non-leak across caller types.

multiple shell-injection payload shapes must each fail to produce execution-output text in the ping tool's response body — test at minimum a semicolon-appended command, a pipe-chained command, and an ampersand-chained command, and for each assert the full response string contains no execution artifact or command output; conclude the same test with a benign target call and assert an output field is present.

create a task under one team with a unique sentinel title, then in the same test call search_tasks as the creating team's member and assert the sentinel title appears in the response body; call search_tasks with a token from a different team using the same query and assert the sentinel title is absent from the response body — both assertions must be raw-body content checks, not merely list-length comparisons.

after calling delete_task on a task, call search_tasks with that task's unique title and assert the response body does not contain the title; then call update_task on the same deleted task id and assert a Not Found or error response — the search-empty content proof and the update-not-found assertion must both appear in the same test, confirming deletion propagates across tool surfaces.

scoring_notes: Tests that only check exceptions without validating output/state will receive low scores.
Tests must combine setup + action + verification steps.
Tests that reuse results across tools (chaining) score higher.
Tests must include both attacker and legitimate user flows.
Failure to include multi-caller scenarios results in major penalty.
